1: 'Woe to the rebellious children, declares the LORD, who carry out plans but not mine, who make alliances but not by my Spirit, piling sin upon sin' — the Egypt-alliance woe against Hezekiah's court: 'hoi banim sorerim, woe to rebellious children' (sorerim = the stubborn/rebellious — from sarar = to be stubborn, the same root as Deuteronomy 21:18-21's 'stubborn and rebellious son' who refuses parental discipline and is brought to the elders for judgment). 'Who carry out plans but not mine' (laasos etsah velo minni, doing counsel but not from me). 'Who make alliances' (linsokh massekhah, pouring out a treaty-libation = the covenant-sealing ceremony of pouring a libation) 'but not by my Spirit' (velo ruchi = not my Spirit) — the political treaty-making done without prophetic (Spirit) consultation. 'Piling sin upon sin' (lemaan sefet chata al-chata, to add sin upon sin = the compounding of one apostasy with another).
2: 'Who set off to go down to Egypt without asking me, who seek to find strength in Pharaoh's power and to take shelter in Egypt's shadow' — the specific failure of prophetic consultation: 'larekhet larekhed Mitsrayim uvefay lo sha'alu, who walk to go down to Egypt but my mouth they did not consult' (sha'alu = inquire/ask, the technical term for consulting YHWH through a prophet: Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 28:6; Isaiah 30:8). 'To find strength in Pharaoh's power' (lammoz bemo'oz Para'oh, to take refuge in Pharaoh's strength). 'To take shelter in Egypt's shadow' (velachsot betseil Mitsrayim, to take shelter in Egypt's shadow) — the 'shadow' (tsel) as protective shade echoes Numbers 14:9 ('their protection is gone') and Psalms 121:5 ('the LORD watches over you — the LORD is your shade'). Pharaoh's shadow vs. YHWH's shadow — the misplaced security.
3: 'Therefore the strength of Pharaoh will become your shame, and the shelter of Egypt's shadow your humiliation' — the reversal of the shelter: 'vehayah lakhem maoz Para'oh levoshet, the strength of Pharaoh will become your shame' — the very thing they sought as protection (maoz = stronghold/refuge) becomes the source of shame (boshet). 'And the shelter of Egypt's shadow your humiliation' (vehechasut betsel Mitsrayim lekhelimmah, and the shelter in Egypt's shadow becomes disgrace). The political calculation is inverted: trusting Egypt brings not security but shame. This is Isaiah's core prophetic critique: the honor-shame reversal where the high-status ally (Egypt) becomes the source of dishonor rather than the protection they sought.
4: 'For though his officials are stationed at Zoan and his envoys have reached as far as Hanes' — the geographical detail of the Judean embassy to Egypt: 'ki-hayu besoan saraiv, for his officers/officials were at Zoan.' Zoan (Hebrew: Tsoan = Tanis/San el-Hagar in the Nile Delta) was the Egyptian capital under the 25th (Ethiopian/Kushite) Dynasty and 26th (Saite) Dynasty — the seat of Pharaoh's power and the site where diplomatic negotiations would be conducted. 'Vemalakaiv chanes yaggi'u, and his envoys reached as far as Hanes' — Hanes (possibly Herakleopolis Magna/modern Ihnasya el-Medina, further south in Middle Egypt) shows the extent of the diplomatic mission. The historical precision: real Judean embassies to real Egyptian court locations.
5: 'Everyone will be ashamed because of a nation that cannot help them — no advantage, no aid, only shame and disgrace' — the universal shame of the Egypt policy: 'kol hevish al-am lo-yo'ilu lahem, all will be put to shame over a people who cannot benefit them.' 'No advantage' (lo le'ezer, not for help) 'no aid' (velo lemo'il, not for benefit) 'only shame and disgrace' (ki velimhah velegam cherpah, but for shame and also for reproach). The Egyptian alliance, which was designed to enhance Judah's geopolitical status, produces the precise opposite: the shame (bosheth, the public humiliation of having backed the wrong partner) and disgrace (cherpah, the reproach of a failed strategy). This is the honor-shame dynamic of ANE politics: alliance failure brought shame on both the vassal who sought the alliance and their patron who failed to deliver.
6: 'The oracle of the beasts of the Negeb' — the Negev route oracle: 'massa behemot negev, the oracle of the beasts of the Negev/south.' This unique prophetic title introduces a description of the dangerous southern trade route to Egypt. 'Through a land of trouble and anguish, of lioness and lion, of viper and flying serpent' (be'erets tsarah vetsukah, lavi va'aryeh mimmennah, efeh vesaraf meoeph, in a land of trouble and difficulty, lioness and lion from there, viper and flying serpent) — the specific fauna of the Negev-Sinai desert corridor that caravans would traverse. The 'flying serpent' (saraf meoeph) appears in 14:29 and Numbers 21:6-9 (the serpents in the wilderness). 'They carry their wealth on the backs of donkeys and their treasures on the humps of camels, to a people that cannot help them' — the tribute-gift Hezekiah sent to secure Egyptian alliance (2 Kings 18:14-16: 'Hezekiah stripped the silver from the doors of the temple and gave it all to the king of Assyria'; similar tribute to Egypt is implied).
7: 'For Egypt's help is worthless and empty. Therefore I call her Rahab Who Sits Still' — the definitive characterization of Egypt's uselessness: 'veMitsrayim hevel varik ya'azoru, Egypt's help is emptiness and nothingness, therefore I cried for her Rahab Who Sits Still.' 'Hevel varik' (emptiness and nothingness — hevel = vanity/breath/vapor, the key word of Ecclesiastes; varik = empty/worthless) = the most dismissive possible description of Egypt's military power. 'Rahab Who Sits Still' (Rahab hem shevet) — Rahab is the ANE chaos-dragon/sea-monster (Job 9:13: 'God does not restrain his anger; even the cohorts of Rahab cowered beneath him'; Isaiah 26:12; Psalms 87:4: 'I will record Rahab and Babylon among those who acknowledge me'). The chaos-dragon of mythology — applied as a mocking nickname to Egypt = the monster who can't even get up, who just sits there. The nickname exposes Egypt's imperial pretension: a mythological monster who is actually paralyzed.
8: 'Now go, write it before them on a tablet and inscribe it in a book, that it may serve as a witness for the days to come, forever and ever' — the witnessing commission: 'attah bo ketevah al-luach itam, now come, write it on a tablet with them.' Writing the oracle on a tablet (luach = a stone or wooden tablet) 'and in a book inscribe it' (ve'al sefer chuqequah, and on a scroll engrave it). The double inscription: tablet (public, permanent) and book (preserved, portable). 'Vattehi leyom acharon la'ad ad-olam, that it may be for the latter day, for ever and ever' — the prophetic testimony preserved as a legal witness against the generation that rejected it (cf. Deuteronomy 31:19: 'write down this song and teach it to the Israelites... it will be a witness for me against them'). Isaiah 8:16-17 contains the same sealing-of-testimony motif.
9: 'For this is a rebellious people, deceptive children — children who refuse to hear the instruction of the LORD' — the covenant-violation diagnosis: 'ki am meri hu, for it is a rebellious people' (am meri, a people of rebellion — meri = obstinate rebellion, from marah = to be contentious/rebellious against authority, the same root as the name Meribah/Massah in the wilderness). 'Banim kechashim, deceptive children' (banim = children of the covenant; kechashim = those who lie/fail, from kachash = to deny/prove false). 'Lo avu shmoa torat YHWH, who refuse to hear the instruction of YHWH' — the willful refusal of Torah (torat YHWH, the instruction/law of YHWH). The parent-child rebellion language (banim sorerim in Isaiah 30:1; banim kechashim here) = the covenant relationship described through the family metaphor, with YHWH as the parent whose children are rebellious.
10: 'Who say to the seers, Stop seeing, and to the prophets, Don't prophesy truth to us — speak flattering things, prophesy delusions' — the demand for comfortable prophecy: 'la'asher amru laro'im lo tir'u, who say to the seers: do not see.' The ro'im (seers = the older title for prophets, 1 Samuel 9:9: 'the one now called a prophet was formerly called a seer'). 'And to the prophets: do not prophesy to us truths' (valanevim, lo tenevu lanu nesharot, to the prophets: do not prophesy straight/right things). 'Speak flattering things' (divru lanu chalaqot, speak smooth/slippery things to us) — chalaqot = the smooth, flattering speech that goes down easily (Proverbs 5:3: 'the lips of the adulteress drip honey'). 'Prophesy delusions' (chitzu machazot, prophesy illusions/deceptions). The demand for false prophecy has a long ANE parallel: court prophets who prophesy what kings want to hear (1 Kings 22:6-23: Ahab's 400 prophets vs. Micaiah).
11: 'Get out of the road, turn aside from the path; let us hear no more from the Holy One of Israel' — the radical rejection of prophetic ministry: 'suru min-haderekh, turn out of the way.' The 'way' (derekh) is the prophetic path of Torah-instruction. 'Turn aside from the path' (hattu min-hanaytiv, turn aside from the path/trodden-road). 'Let us hear no more from the Holy One of Israel' (chadilu mippaneinu qedosh Yisrael, cease from before us the Holy One of Israel) — the explicit rejection of YHWH's distinctive prophetic identity: qedosh Yisrael (the Holy One of Israel) appears 25 times in Isaiah and is virtually his prophetic signature. The people demand that this God cease to speak — they want a prophet without a Holy One of Israel behind him.
12: 'Therefore thus says the Holy One of Israel: Because you reject this word and place your trust in oppression and cunning and rely on them' — the judicial 'therefore' after the rejection of prophetic counsel: 'lakhen ko-amar qedosh Yisrael, therefore thus says the Holy One of Israel' — the divine judge pronounces sentence. 'Ya'an ma'askhem et-haddavar hazeh, because you rejected this word.' 'And placed your trust in oppression' (vattevetchu be'oshqe, and you trusted in oppression/exploitation = the political manipulation and exploitation of the weak that characterized the court's policy). 'And cunning' (uvenalutz, and in deviance/twistedness = the diplomatic twisting/scheming of the Egypt alliance). The double trust (betach = deep reliance, the technical covenant-trust word used for misplaced confidence) — they trusted oppression and cunning instead of YHWH.
13: 'Therefore this sin will be for you like a crack in a high wall, bulging out, about to collapse — whose fall comes suddenly, in an instant' — the crumbling-wall simile: 'vihyeh lakhem haavon hazeh kepherets nopel, and this iniquity will be for you like a falling breach.' 'Naspeh begadah shomah, bulging out in a high wall' (naspeh from saphah = puffed out/swelling; gadah = the high/towering wall). The bulging crack in the high wall: the imperceptible initial damage that suddenly becomes catastrophic collapse. 'Asher pith'om lefeta yavo shibrah, whose shattering comes suddenly, in an instant' — the same 'suddenly, in an instant' (pith'om lefeta, cf. Isaiah 29:5: 'in an instant, suddenly') of divine judgment. The timing of structural failure: the crack forms slowly and invisibly, the collapse comes without warning. The Egypt alliance is that crack.
14: 'It will shatter like a potter's vessel smashed beyond all saving — no fragment left large enough to carry a coal from the fire or scoop water from a cistern' — the total destruction of the potter's vessel: 'ushevira keshiver nevel yotsrim, and its breaking will be like the breaking of a potter's vessel shattered.' The nevel (jug/wine-jar) — a clay vessel whose shattering leaves no usable fragment. 'Lashmutz lo yachmal, he will not spare in its crushing' (lachmus, for compassion/sparing). 'Velo yimmatse vishshevatah, and in its fragments will not be found.' The standard of utter destruction: 'A coal from the fire' (gachalat esh) and 'water from a cistern' (mayim migeve) — the two basic survival tools (fire for warmth/cooking, water for drinking). Even these minimal-survival fragments are absent. Jeremiah 19:1-11 uses the same shattered-potter's-vessel imagery for Jerusalem.
15: 'For thus said the Lord GOD, the Holy One of Israel: In repentance and rest you shall be saved; in quietness and in trust shall be your strength. But you were not willing' — the great text of prophetic soteriology: 'bishuva venachat tivvaashe'un, in repentance/returning and rest you shall be saved.' The shuvah (returning = repentance, the turning of the covenant-breaker back to YHWH; Jeremiah 3:12-14: 'Return, faithless Israel') and nachah (the rest/quiet of ceasing from military activity = demobilization, trusting YHWH rather than armies). 'In quietness and trust shall be your strength' (beshaqat uvetivtacha tihyeh gevuratkhem, in quietness and in trust shall be your strength) — the gerurah (strength/might) comes from the attitude of quiet trust (betach), not from military power. 'But you were not willing' (velo avitem, but you refused) — the short devastating conclusion. The invitation refused, the salvation not received.
16: 'And you said, No! We will escape on horses — and so you will flee! We will ride on swift horses — and so those who pursue you will be swift!' — the ironic wordplay of the military rejection: 'vattemru lo ki al-sus nanus, and you said, No! For on a horse we will flee.' The cavalry strategy: 'We will escape/flee' (nanus) — the word for military retreat also means 'escape' — the cavalry is for fleeing from danger. 'Al-qal nirkav, on swift horses we will ride' — the emphasis on speed (qal = swift/light). The ironic reversal: 'therefore swift will be your pursuer' (lakhen yiqallu rodeikhem, therefore your pursuers will be swift). The speed they trust (the fast horse) is matched by faster pursuit. The faster you are, the faster your enemy will need to be — and YHWH gives the enemy the needed speed.
17: 'A thousand will flee at the threat of one; at the threat of five you will all run, until you are left like a flagstaff on a mountaintop, like a signal flag on a hill' — the covenant-curse ratio of defeat: 'alep echad mipenei ge'arat echad, a thousand will flee at the threat of one.' Leviticus 26:8 reverses this for blessing: 'five of you will chase a hundred, and a hundred will chase ten thousand' — the blessing of military power multiplies the few against the many. Here the curse-ratio inverts it: the many flee from the few. 'Until you are left like a flagstaff on a mountaintop' (ad-im nothartem katorren al-rosh hahar, until you are left like a mast on the top of the mountain) — the lone flagpole (niqreh in Isaiah 30:17, torenm = ship's mast/flagpole) standing on an empty mountaintop: the last survivor of the army, alone and exposed, serving only as a landmark.
18: 'Therefore the LORD waits to be gracious to you, and therefore he rises up to show you mercy. For the LORD is a God of justice; blessed are all who wait for him' — the pivot from judgment to grace: 'velakhen yechakkeh YHWH lachannenkhem, and therefore YHWH waits to be gracious to you.' The divine waiting (yechakkeh, from chakah = to wait/long for) mirrors the human waiting commanded throughout Isaiah. YHWH himself waits for the opportune moment to show grace — the waiting is not absence but readiness. 'He rises up to show you mercy' (veyarum lerachamekhem, and he rises to have compassion on you). 'For YHWH is a God of justice' (ki-elohei mishpat YHWH, for a God of justice is YHWH) — the justice (mishpat) that requires the exile also requires the restoration when the exile is complete. 'Blessed are all who wait for him' (ashre kol-chokhei lo) = the beatitude of the waiting community (Psalms 37:9: 'those who wait for the LORD will inherit the land').
19: 'For the people of Zion who dwell in Jerusalem — you will weep no more. He will surely be gracious to you at the sound of your cry; as soon as he hears it, he will answer you' — the specific promise to the Zion community: 'ki-am beTsiyon yoshev biYerushalayim, for the people of Zion, the one dwelling in Jerusalem.' The promise: 'lo-tivkeh bakho, you will weep no more — weeping you will not weep' (the emphatic absolute infinitive bako = weeping, weep). The instant responsiveness: 'channon yechannekha leqol za'aqekha, he will surely be gracious to you at the sound of your cry' (za'aqekha = your outcry, the cry of distress that YHWH heard from Egypt in Exodus 3:7: 'I have heard the cry of my people'). 'As soon as he hears it, he will answer you' (keshomeo anekha, when he hears, he will answer you) — the immediacy of divine response to genuine prayer.
20: 'And though the Lord gives you the bread of adversity and the water of affliction, your Teacher will no longer hide himself, but your eyes will see your Teacher' — the paradox of the teaching-in-affliction: 'veten lakhem adonay lechem tsar umayim lachats, and the Lord gives you bread of adversity and water of oppression.' The bread (lechem) and water (mayim) of adversity/affliction = the reduced rations of siege conditions (cf. 1 Kings 22:27: 'Feed this man nothing but bread and water until I return safely' — prison rations). Even in these reduced conditions: 'your Teacher will no longer hide himself' (velo-yikkannef od moreka, and your Teacher will no longer hide himself). The Moreh (Teacher = from yarah, to teach; same root as Torah) who had been concealed (the sealed book, the spirit of deep sleep of Isaiah 29:10-12) now appears. 'Your eyes will see your Teacher' — the direct vision of the divine instructor in the age of fulfillment.
21: 'And your ears will hear a voice behind you saying, This is the way; walk in it, whether you turn to the right or to the left' — the guiding voice of the Teacher: 've'azneikha tishmena davar me'acharekha, and your ears will hear a word behind you saying.' The 'voice behind' (me'acharekha = from behind you) suggests the shepherd guiding the flock from behind (cf. Genesis 32:7 where Jacob puts servants, flocks, and herds ahead of him; the shepherd driving from behind). 'This is the way; walk in it' (zeh haderekh, lekhu-vo, this is the way, walk in it) — the specific directional instruction for each moment of decision (whether turning right or left). The derekh (way/road) as the covenant path (Psalms 16:11: 'you make known to me the path of life'; Proverbs 3:6: 'in all your ways acknowledge him and he will make your paths straight'). John 14:6 echoes this in Jesus's 'I am the way.'
22: 'Then you will treat as defiled the silver coating of your carved idols and the gold plating of your cast images; you will throw them away like a menstrual rag, saying to them, Away with you!' — the spontaneous idol-rejection of the restored community: 'vetimme'tem et-tsippui pesele kaspekhm, and you will defile the plating of your silver carved idols.' The timmei (defiling) = treating as unclean (tamei, the ritual-impurity vocabulary). The gold-and-silver coated wooden idols were precious objects — the spontaneous rejection ('Away with you!' = tse = go out!) signals a fundamental change in the community's orientation. 'Like a menstrual rag' (kemo davah, like a menstruant = the most unclean object in the purity system, Leviticus 15:19-30) — the precious idol becomes as repugnant as the most defiling object imaginable. The same idol-rejection as Isaiah 2:20; Isaiah 17:7-8; Isaiah 31:7.
23: 'He will send rain for the seed you sow in the ground; the bread from the ground will be rich and plentiful. In that day your livestock will graze in spacious pastures' — the agricultural blessing of covenant restoration: 'venatan geshem zar'akha asher tizra, and he will give rain for your seed which you sow.' Rain for the seed = the fundamental blessing of Leviticus 26:4 ('I will send you rain in its season, and the ground will yield its crops') and Deuteronomy 28:12 ('the LORD will open the heavens... to send rain on your land in season'). The land blessing reverses the covenant curse: Deuteronomy 28:23-24 ('the sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath you iron; the LORD will turn the rain of your country into dust and powder'). 'Rich and plentiful bread' (lechem tenuva ha'adamah veshamen vedashemn, bread, the produce of the ground, rich and fat). 'Spacious pastures' (karkareh rachavah, a wide meadow) for the livestock.
24: 'The oxen and donkeys that work the ground will eat salted fodder, which has been winnowed clean with shovel and fork' — the agricultural prosperity extending to working animals: 'haalapim vehacharim ovede ha'adamah belil chamits yochelu, the oxen and donkeys that work the ground will eat salted/fermented fodder.' The working animals (oxen for plowing and threshing; donkeys for transport) — the lowest tier of the agricultural system — receive special care in the restoration. 'Belil chamits' (fermented/salted fodder) = the high-quality feed of grain treated with salt or vinegar fermentation, better than plain dried grass. 'Winnowed clean with shovel and fork' (zureh bera'shan uvamizreh) = carefully processed fodder, not the rough unprocessed kind. The attention to the quality of the working animals' food signals the completeness of the blessing: even the animals eat well.
25: 'And on every high mountain and every lofty hill there will be streams and flowing channels, on the day of the great slaughter when the towers fall' — the water-blessing on the heights: 'vehayah al-kol-har gavoah ve'al kol-giv'ah nissa yihyu pelagim yevlei mayim, and on every high mountain and every lofty hill there will be streams and channels of water.' In the dry climate of the Levant, water flows in valleys (wadis) not on mountaintops — streams on every high mountain = a miraculous super-abundance of water reversing geography. This is the new-creation imagery of water in unexpected places (cf. Isaiah 35:6-7: 'water will gush in the wilderness; streams in the desert'). 'In the day of the great slaughter when the towers fall' — the military defeat of the enemy (Assyrian towers) is simultaneous with the agricultural blessing for the covenant community.
26: 'Moreover the light of the moon will be like the light of the sun, and the light of the sun will be seven times stronger — as bright as seven days — in the day when the LORD binds up the wound of his people and heals the blow he has struck them' — the amplified luminaries of the new age: 'vehayah or halevana keor hachammah, and the light of the moon will be like the light of the sun' — the moon, which gives reflected light approximately one-millionth of the sun's brightness, will shine as brightly as the current sun. 'And the light of the sun will be sevenfold, like the light of seven days' (veor hachammah yihyeh shiv'atayim keor shiv'at hayamim, the light of the sun will be seven times, like the light of seven days). Seven = completeness; seven days = the full creation-week. Revelation 21:23 and 22:5 fulfill this: 'the city does not need the sun or moon, for the glory of God gives it light.' 'When YHWH binds up the wound of his people' — the healing alongside the amplified light.
27: 'See, the name of the LORD comes from far away, burning with anger and heavy with rising smoke; his lips are filled with fury and his tongue is like a consuming fire' — the theophanic approach of YHWH from afar: 'hineh shem YHWH ba mimerachok, behold the name of YHWH is coming from far away.' 'The name of YHWH' = YHWH himself, his self-disclosure and presence (the name as the person in ANE thought: to invoke the name is to invoke the person). 'Comes from far away' (mimerachok) — the divine warrior approaching from his heavenly dwelling (Habakkuk 3:3: 'God came from Teman, the Holy One from Mount Paran; his glory covered the heavens'). The four theophanic features: burning anger (choleh apo, his anger burning), heavy smoke (vekoved massa, heavy with the burden of smoke), lips filled with fury (ume'lei za'am sefataiv), tongue like consuming fire (veleshono ke'esh okheleth, his tongue like eating fire).
28: 'His breath is like a flooding stream reaching to the neck, to sift the nations through a sieve of destruction, and to put a misleading bridle on the jaws of the peoples' — the divine breath as the agent of judgment: 'verucho keanahal shotef, his breath/spirit is like a flooding stream' (shotef = the overwhelming/overflowing — the same Assyrian-flood imagery of Isaiah 8:7-8; Isaiah 10:22; Isaiah 28:15-18 now applied to YHWH's own breath). 'Ad-tsavar, reaching to the neck' = the Assyrian flood reaching to the neck (Isaiah 8:8: 'it will overflow all its channels, sweeping into Judah, swirling over it, reaching up to the neck'). 'To sift the nations with a sieve of destruction' (lehanafat goyim benifat shav, to sift nations with a sieve of destruction/futility) — the nations sifted like grain through a sieve, removing the chaff. 'And a misleading bridle on the jaws of the peoples' (verimasekhet meviyah al-lechi ammim) = a bit/bridle in the mouths of the nations that causes them to go where YHWH intends, not where they plan.
29: 'You will have a song, like the song sung at night when a holy feast is kept, and gladness of heart, like one who marches to the sound of a flute to go up to the mountain of the LORD, to the Rock of Israel' — the night-festival song of the pilgrimage community: 'hashir yihyeh lakhem keleyl hitqaddesh chag, the song will be yours like the night of the sanctification of a feast.' The night-song = the Passover night (Exodus 12:42: 'a night of watching for the LORD'), when the entire celebration begins at night. 'Gladness of heart like one who marches to the sound of a flute to go up to the mountain of YHWH' (simchat lev kaholek bechalil, the gladness of heart like walking with a flute) — the joyful pilgrimage procession to the temple (Psalms 42:4: 'how I used to go with the multitude, leading the procession to the house of God, with shouts of joy and praise'). 'The Rock of Israel' (tsur Yisrael) = YHWH as Rock (Isaiah 26:4; 2 Samuel 23:3).
30: 'The LORD will make his majestic voice heard and make visible the descent of his arm in raging anger and in the flame of a consuming fire — a cloudburst, storm, and hailstones' — the audible and visible theophany: 'vehishmia YHWH et-hod qolo, and YHWH will make heard the majesty of his voice.' The hod (majesty/thunder = the royal splendor of YHWH's voice, Psalms 29:4: 'the voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic'). 'And the descent of his arm' (venahath zero'o yar'eh, and the descent of his arm he will show) — the arm (zero'a) as the instrument of power (Exodus 6:6: 'with an outstretched arm I will bring you out from Egypt'). The four weather-phenomena of the theophany: za'am (indignation/fury) + lehav esh okhelah (flame of consuming fire) + negeph (a cloudburst/plague) + zu'ah (storm/tempest) + even barad (hailstone).
31: 'For at the voice of the LORD the Assyrian will be shattered; with his rod he will strike him down' — the specific prediction of Assyrian defeat by divine voice: 'ki-miqol YHWH yechat Ashshur, for at the voice of YHWH Assyria will be shattered.' The 'voice of YHWH' (qol YHWH) as the weapon that destroys Assyria — no human army needed. The fulfillment: 2 Kings 19:35/Isaiah 37:36: 'the angel of the LORD went out and put to death 185,000 Assyrian soldiers.' 'With his rod he will strike him down' (beshebet yakkeh, with a rod/scepter he will strike) — the same shebet (rod) that YHWH used to punish Israel through Assyria (Isaiah 10:5: 'the rod of my anger, the staff in whose hand is my wrath') is now used against Assyria itself. The divine rod that punishes is sovereign and can be redirected against the punishing agent.
32: 'And every blow of the rod of punishment that the LORD lays on him will be accompanied by tambourines and lyres; and with brandished arm he will battle against them' — the celebratory defeat: 'vehayah kol-maavar matteh musdah asher yaniyach YHWH alaw betupim uvekhinnorot, and it will be that every blow of the ordained rod which YHWH lays on it is with tambourines and lyres.' The matteh musdah (the rod of the founding/ordaining = the rod that has been established/appointed for this purpose) — the divine appointment of the instrument of punishment. The tambourines (tupim) and lyres (kinnorot) accompanying each blow = the instruments of the victory celebration (Exodus 15:20: Miriam's tambourine after the Exodus; Psalms 149:3: 'praise him with tambourine and dancing'). Each blow of divine judgment on Assyria is simultaneously a beat in the celebration-music of the redeemed.
33: 'For Topheth has long been prepared; indeed, it is made ready for the king. Its pyre is deep and wide, with fire and wood in abundance; the breath of the LORD kindles it like a stream of burning sulfur' — the Topheth-prepared oracle: 'ki-aruch mete'mol Topheth, for Topheth has long been arranged.' Topheth (the Tophet/Topheth = from taphat = fireplace/hearth; or possibly from the Hebrew word for 'drum' = toph, used to drown out the cries of children sacrificed there) was the cultic site in the Valley of Ben Hinnom (ge-hinnom = Gehenna) south of Jerusalem where Israel burned children as offerings to Molech (Jeremiah 7:31-32; Isaiah 19:5-6; Isaiah 32:35; 2 Kings 23:10). YHWH has prepared Topheth — traditionally the site of pagan sacrifice — for the Assyrian king (hammelek, the king). 'The breath of YHWH kindles it like a stream of burning sulfur' (nishmat YHWH ke'nahal gofrit bo'eret bah, the breath of YHWH like a stream of burning sulfur kindles it) — the divine breath (Isaiah 30:28) now the fuel of the Assyrian king's burning in the Gehenna-site.
